spec_text:
============================================================
IMPLEMENTATION SPEC: orders.py
============================================================
Single file. No imports outside Python stdlib. No persistence.
No threading. No external dependencies. Pure in-memory.

============================================================
1. IMPORTS (top of file)
============================================================
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import itertools

============================================================
2. PUBLIC EXPORTS (must all be importable from orders)
============================================================
- OrderStatus              (Enum)
- Order                    (class / dataclass)
- OrderHistory             (class)
- OrderService             (class)
- InvalidTransitionError   (Exception subclass)
- OrderNotFoundError       (Exception subclass)
- InvalidOrderError        (Exception subclass)

Define each at module top level. Optionally include
__all__ = ["OrderStatus", "Order", "OrderHistory",
           "OrderService", "InvalidTransitionError",
           "OrderNotFoundError", "InvalidOrderError"]

============================================================
3. EXCEPTIONS
============================================================
Each is a plain subclass of Exception with no extra body:

class InvalidTransitionError(Exception):
    pass

class OrderNotFoundError(Exception):
    pass

class InvalidOrderError(Exception):
    pass

CRITICAL: They must be DISTINCT exception types. Do not
alias them. Do not make one inherit from another. Tests will
likely use pytest.raises(InvalidTransitionError) etc. and
will fail if the wrong type is raised.

============================================================
4. OrderStatus (Enum)
============================================================
class OrderStatus(Enum):
    NEW = "NEW"
    PAID = "PAID"
    SHIPPED = "SHIPPED"
    DELIVERED = "DELIVERED"
    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"

CRITICAL PITFALL: Tests will compare with the enum member
itself, e.g. `order.status == OrderStatus.PAID`. NEVER store
status as a string. Always store the OrderStatus member.
The .value can be anything; "NEW"/"PAID"/etc. is fine.

============================================================
5. MODULE-LEVEL CONSTANTS
============================================================
# Allowed transitions: source_status -> set of target statuses.
# None is the implicit pre-creation source for NEW.
ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS: dict[Optional[OrderStatus], set[OrderStatus]] = {
    None: {OrderStatus.NEW},
    OrderStatus.NEW: {OrderStatus.PAID, OrderStatus.CANCELLED},
    OrderStatus.PAID: {OrderStatus.SHIPPED, OrderStatus.CANCELLED},
    OrderStatus.SHIPPED: {OrderStatus.DELIVERED},
    OrderStatus.DELIVERED: set(),
    OrderStatus.CANCELLED: set(),
}

(You don't have to use this table directly if you prefer
inline checks, but the transition rules MUST match it.)

============================================================
6. Order (dataclass)
============================================================
@dataclass
class Order:
    id: str
    customer_id: str
    items: list[tuple[str, float, int]]
    status: OrderStatus = OrderStatus.NEW
    total: float = 0.0   # populated in __post_init__

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        # Validate customer_id
        if not isinstance(self.customer_id, str) or self.customer_id == "":
            raise InvalidOrderError("customer_id must be a non-empty string")

        # Validate items list
        if not isinstance(self.items, list) or len(self.items) == 0:
            raise InvalidOrderError("items must be a non-empty list")

        for item in self.items:
            # Each item must be a tuple of (name, price, quantity)
            if not (isinstance(item, tuple) and len(item) == 3):
                raise InvalidOrderError(
                    "each item must be a (name, price, quantity) tuple"
                )
            name, price, quantity = item
            if not isinstance(name, str) or name == "":
                raise InvalidOrderError("item name must be a non-empty string")
            # bool is a subclass of int — reject explicitly to be safe
            if isinstance(price, bool) or not isinstance(price, (int, float)):
                raise InvalidOrderError("item price must be numeric")
            if price <= 0:
                raise InvalidOrderError("item price must be > 0")
            if isinstance(quantity, bool) or not isinstance(quantity, int):
                raise InvalidOrderError("item quantity must be an int")
            if quantity < 1:
                raise InvalidOrderError("item quantity must be >= 1")

        # Compute total
        self.total = float(sum(price * quantity
                               for _, price, quantity in self.items))

NOTES:
- `status` defaults to OrderStatus.NEW. Do NOT default to a string.
- `total` is a regular attribute computed in __post_init__,
  NOT a @property. The README says `Order.total returns the
  correct sum`; an attribute satisfies this. (A @property is
  also acceptable — but if you use one, do NOT also have
  `total` as a dataclass field.)
- Recompute total ONLY in __post_init__. Do not recompute
  on attribute access; the items list is treated as fixed
  after construction.

============================================================
7. OrderHistory
============================================================
class OrderHistory:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        # Map order_id -> list of transition dicts
        self._records: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}
        # Monotonically-increasing sequence counter shared across
        # all orders, so ordering is well-defined.
        self._seq = itertools.count(1)

    def record(self,
               order_id: str,
               from_status: Optional[OrderStatus],
               to_status: OrderStatus) -> None:
        entry = {
            "from_status": from_status,   # OrderStatus or None
            "to_status": to_status,       # OrderStatus
            "timestamp": next(self._seq), # int, monotonically increasing
        }
        self._records.setdefault(order_id, []).append(entry)

    def get_transitions(self, order_id: str) -> list[dict]:
        # Unknown order id returns []. Do NOT raise.
        # Return a shallow copy so callers cannot mutate internal state.
        return list(self._records.get(order_id, []))

CRITICAL PITFALLS:
- Dict keys MUST be exactly: "from_status", "to_status",
  "timestamp". Tests will index by these strings.
- `from_status` MUST be the OrderStatus member (or None for
  the initial creation transition). Do NOT convert to string.
- `to_status` MUST be the OrderStatus member. Do NOT
  convert to string.
- Timestamps must be strictly increasing across all calls
  to record(). Using itertools.count satisfies this.
  datetime.now() is risky on fast machines (same microsecond).
  Prefer the integer sequence.
- Unknown id => [], NOT raise.

============================================================
8. OrderService
============================================================
class OrderService:
    def __init__(self, history: OrderHistory) -> None:
        self._history = history
        self._orders: dict[str, Order] = {}
        # ID generator for newly placed orders.
        self._id_seq = itertools.count(1)

    # ---- order id helper ---------------------------------
    def _new_id(self) -> str:
        return f"ord-{next(self._id_seq)}"

    # ---- lookup helper -----------------------------------
    def _get(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
        if order_id not in self._orders:
            raise OrderNotFoundError(f"unknown order id: {order_id}")
        return self._orders[order_id]

    # ---- transition helper -------------------------------
    def _transition(self, order: Order, target: OrderStatus) -> None:
        allowed = ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS.get(order.status, set())
        if target not in allowed:
            raise InvalidTransitionError(
                f"cannot transition from {order.status} to {target}"
            )
        from_status = order.status
        order.status = target
        self._history.record(order.id, from_status, target)

    # ---- REQ-2: place_order ------------------------------
    def place_order(self,
                    customer_id: str,
                    items: list[tuple[str, float, int]]) -> Order:
        # Order(...) raises InvalidOrderError on bad input — let it bubble.
        order = Order(
            id=self._new_id(),
            customer_id=customer_id,
            items=items,
            status=OrderStatus.NEW,
        )
        self._orders[order.id] = order
        # Initial transition: None -> NEW
        self._history.record(order.id, None, OrderStatus.NEW)
        return order

    # ---- REQ-3: mark_paid --------------------------------
    def mark_paid(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)              # OrderNotFoundError
        self._transition(order, OrderStatus.PAID)  # InvalidTransitionError

    # ---- REQ-4: ship -------------------------------------
    def ship(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)
        self._transition(order, OrderStatus.SHIPPED)

    # ---- REQ-4: deliver ----------------------------------
    def deliver(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)
        self._transition(order, OrderStatus.DELIVERED)

    # ---- REQ-5: cancel -----------------------------------
    def cancel(self, order_id: str) -> None:
        order = self._get(order_id)
        # Only NEW or PAID may cancel.
        if order.status not in (OrderStatus.NEW, OrderStatus.PAID):
            raise InvalidTransitionError(
                f"cannot cancel order in status {order.status}"
            )
        self._transition(order, OrderStatus.CANCELLED)

    # ---- (optional) get_order ----------------------------
    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
        return self._get(order_id)

============================================================
9. ORDER OF OPERATIONS / SEQUENCING RULES
============================================================
- Lookup-failure (OrderNotFoundError) MUST be checked BEFORE
  transition-validity (InvalidTransitionError). I.e. given
  an unknown id, raise OrderNotFoundError, never
  InvalidTransitionError.
- For place_order: input validation happens inside Order
  __post_init__. If invalid, InvalidOrderError is raised
  BEFORE the order is stored and BEFORE any history entry
  is recorded.
- For successful transitions: history is recorded AFTER
  the order's status field is updated, but the from_status
  passed to history MUST be the PRE-transition value.
- One history entry per successful state change. Failed
  transitions MUST NOT record anything.

============================================================
10. CRITICAL PITFALLS — READ TWICE
============================================================
1. Tests compare with the enum: `order.status == OrderStatus.PAID`.
   Storing/returning the string "PAID" will fail. Use the
   OrderStatus member everywhere — in Order.status, in
   history "from_status"/"to_status", and in the
   ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS table.

2. `from_status` for the FIRST history entry of a placed
   order is None (Python None), NOT OrderStatus.NEW, NOT
   the string "None".

3. `OrderHistory.get_transitions` for an unknown id returns
   `[]` and does NOT raise. Other "unknown id" cases on
   OrderService methods DO raise OrderNotFoundError.

4. `cancel()` from CANCELLED, SHIPPED, or DELIVERED MUST
   raise InvalidTransitionError. Do not silently no-op.
   Do not allow CANCELLED -> CANCELLED.

5. Three exception classes are distinct:
   - InvalidOrderError      = malformed input to Order/place_order
   - OrderNotFoundError     = unknown order_id
   - InvalidTransitionError = wrong source status
   Don't collapse them or have one inherit from another.

6. Empty items list raises InvalidOrderError (not ValueError).
   Zero/negative price raises InvalidOrderError.
   Zero/negative quantity raises InvalidOrderError.
   Blank name raises InvalidOrderError.

7. History timestamps must strictly increase across all
   record() calls. Use itertools.count. Do NOT use
   time.time() / datetime.now() — they can collide.

8. The history dict keys MUST be exactly the strings:
   "from_status", "to_status", "timestamp".
   Any other key names will fail tests.

9. `total` must equal sum(price * quantity). For
   `[("a", 9.99, 2), ("b", 1.50, 3)]` total is
   9.99*2 + 1.50*3 = 24.48. Use float arithmetic; do
   not round.

10. OrderService stores orders by id. A second mark_paid()
    on an already-PAID order MUST raise
    InvalidTransitionError (PAID is not in
    ALLOWED_TRANSITIONS[PAID]).

11. Do NOT add features not listed: no persistence, no
    refunds, no partial cancel, no thread locking, no
    logging, no __repr__ customization (dataclass default
    is fine).

12. The single file is named orders.py and contains
    EVERYTHING above. Do not split into multiple modules.

============================================================
11. SELF-CHECK BEFORE EMITTING CODE
============================================================
Before producing the file, mentally verify:
[ ] All 7 names in the public-exports list are defined at
    module top level.
[ ] OrderStatus has exactly 5 members: NEW, PAID, SHIPPED,
    DELIVERED, CANCELLED.
[ ] Order(__post_init__) rejects: empty items, blank
    customer_id, blank name, price <= 0, quantity < 1.
[ ] place_order records (None -> NEW).
[ ] mark_paid: NEW -> PAID only.
[ ] ship:      PAID -> SHIPPED only.
[ ] deliver:   SHIPPED -> DELIVERED only.
[ ] cancel:    NEW or PAID -> CANCELLED; everything else
               raises InvalidTransitionError.
[ ] OrderNotFoundError takes priority over
    InvalidTransitionError on unknown ids.
[ ] OrderHistory.get_transitions("nope") == [].
[ ] Three exception classes are independent subclasses of
    Exception.
[ ] Dict keys in transitions are
    "from_status"/"to_status"/"timestamp".
[ ] `from_status` and `to_status` values are OrderStatus
    members (or None for the very first entry).
